expanded_description: Molar mass is the mass of one mole of a substance, expressed in grams per mole (g/mol), calculated by summing the atomic masses of all atoms in a chemical formula.

core_concept.principle: The molar mass of a substance is numerically equal to its atomic or molecular mass in atomic mass units (amu), but expressed in grams per mole (g/mol).
core_concept.explanation: Because the mole is defined such that $1$ mole of $^{12}\text{C}$ has a mass of exactly $12$ g, the atomic mass of any element (in amu) directly equals its molar mass (in g/mol). For compounds, sum the molar masses of all constituent atoms according to the chemical formula.

image.alt: Diagram showing atomic masses summed to calculate molar mass
image.caption: Molar mass is calculated by summing atomic masses from the periodic table

sections.introduction: Molar mass serves as the conversion factor between the mass of a sample (grams) and the amount of substance (moles). It is one of the most frequently used quantities in chemical calculations.

key_concept.definition: Molar mass ($M$) is the mass in grams of one mole of a substance: $M = \dfrac{m}{n}$ with units $\text{g/mol}$.
key_concept.context: For elements, molar mass is read directly from the periodic table. For compounds, it is calculated by summing the molar masses of all atoms in the formula, accounting for subscripts.

principles.statement: The molar mass of a compound equals the sum of the molar masses of its constituent atoms, each multiplied by its subscript in the chemical formula.
principles.application: This allows calculation of molar mass for any compound from its formula and periodic table data.
principles.analogy: Like calculating the total weight of a package by adding the weights of all items inside, molar mass adds up the 'weights' of all atoms in a formula unit.

components.definition: Molar mass connects mass ($m$), amount in moles ($n$), and chemical identity via the periodic table.
components.interpretation: These three forms allow conversion between any two of mass, moles, or molar mass when the third is known.

image.alt: Triangle showing relationships between mass, moles, and molar mass
image.caption: The molar mass triangle helps organize conversion calculations

worked_examples.title: Molar Mass of an Element
worked_examples.problem: What is the molar mass of iron (Fe), and what mass corresponds to $2.50$ moles of Fe?

step_by_step.step: Find atomic mass on periodic table

step_by_step.explanation: By definition, molar mass = atomic mass numerically, so $M_{\text{Fe}} = 55.85 \text{ g/mol}$
Use $m = n \times M$ to find mass

worked_examples.final_answer: Molar mass of Fe is $55.85$ g/mol; $2.50$ mol of Fe has a mass of $140$ g (3 significant figures).
worked_examples.concept_applied: Elemental molar mass equals atomic mass from periodic table; mass-mole conversions use $m = n \times M$.

sections.key_concept: For compounds, multiply each element's molar mass by its subscript in the formula, then sum all contributions.

classifications.value: Covalently bonded molecules with discrete formula units
characteristics: Molar mass = sum of atomic masses × subscripts
classifications.behavior: Subscripts indicate exact atom counts per molecule

classifications.value: Extended lattice structures represented by empirical formula units
characteristics: Formula represents simplest ratio of ions
Molar mass calculated same way as molecular
classifications.behavior: Subscripts indicate ion ratios in the crystal lattice

categories.description: When formulas contain parentheses, multiply all atoms inside by the external subscript.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Distribute the external subscript to each element within parentheses before summing masses.

worked_examples.title: Molar Mass of a Compound with Parentheses
worked_examples.problem: Calculate the molar mass of magnesium hydroxide, $\text{Mg}(\text{OH})_2$, used in antacids.

step_by_step.explanation: $\text{Mg}(\text{OH})_2$ contains: $1$ Mg, $2$ O, $2$ H (the subscript $2$ applies to both O and H inside parentheses)
From periodic table: Mg = $24.31$ g/mol, O = $16.00$ g/mol, H = $1.008$ g/mol

worked_examples.final_answer: The molar mass of $\text{Mg}(\text{OH})_2$ is $58.33$ g/mol.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Parentheses require distributing subscripts to all enclosed atoms before summing molar masses.

worked_examples.title: Comparing Molar Masses of Related Compounds
worked_examples.problem: Which has a larger molar mass: $\text{CO}$ or $\text{CO}_2$? Calculate both.

step_by_step.explanation: $44.01 > 28.01$, so $\text{CO}_2$ has the larger molar mass

worked_examples.final_answer: $\text{CO}_2$ ($44.01$ g/mol) has a larger molar mass than $\text{CO}$ ($28.01$ g/mol).
worked_examples.concept_applied: Adding atoms to a formula increases molar mass proportionally to their atomic masses.

sections.section_title: Significant Figures in Molar Mass Calculations
sections.introduction: Molar mass values should reflect the precision of the atomic masses used. Follow significant figure rules for addition and multiplication.

key_concept.definition: When summing atomic masses, the result should be reported to the least precise decimal place among the addends.
key_concept.context: Most periodic tables provide atomic masses to two decimal places, so molar masses are typically reported to $0.01$ g/mol precision.

principles.statement: In addition/subtraction, the result has the same number of decimal places as the least precise term.
principles.application: When calculating molar mass, align decimal places and round the final sum appropriately.
principles.example: $12.01$ (2 decimals) + $1.008$ (3 decimals) + $16.00$ (2 decimals) → result rounded to 2 decimals: $29.02$ g/mol
principles.analogy: Like measuring a room with a tape measure marked in centimeters—you can't report millimeter precision if your tool doesn't support it.

worked_examples.title: Applying Significant Figures to Molar Mass
worked_examples.problem: Calculate the molar mass of ethanol, $\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}$ (or $\text{C}_2\text{H}_6\text{O}$), with proper significant figures.

step_by_step.step: Sum and apply significant figure rules
step_by_step.explanation: Add values: $24.02 + 6.048 + 16.00 = 46.068$; least precise terms have 2 decimal places, so round to 2 decimals

worked_examples.final_answer: The molar mass of ethanol is $46.07$ g/mol (reported to two decimal places).
worked_examples.concept_applied: Significant figure rules for addition determine the precision of calculated molar masses.

summary: Molar mass ($M$) is the mass in grams of one mole of a substance, with units g/mol. For elements, it equals the atomic mass from the periodic table. For compounds, calculate molar mass by summing the atomic masses of all atoms in the formula, multiplying each by its subscript and distributing subscripts across parentheses. The relationship $m = n \times M$ enables conversion between mass and moles. Always apply significant figure rules: when adding atomic masses, round the result to the least precise decimal place. Mastery of molar mass calculations is essential for stoichiometry, solution preparation, and all quantitative chemical analysis.
